sentences.text: So I had heard about the Austrian Battalion.
And I was anxious to get into the Army.
I wanted to be there.
And I-- I don't know whether you remember it, but there was a newspaper called a very liberal newspaper.
And they had written about the Austrian Battalion.
And the reason they had written about it is because Archduke Otto and Archduke-- what was the other one?
I forgot the other, his brother-- were in that battalion.
So somehow I had an inkling that I-- and I wanted to be-- I was gung-ho.
I was now a 100% American.
I wanted to forget everything that I ever knew about Vienna and Austria.
And so I got to Camp Upton it was, the staging area.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my cousin went with me to Penn Station and put me on the train.
And I guess my mother, once having to send me was enough, and he-- he-- we became very close.
We lived together, and we're still very close.
So we get to Camp Upton and everybody else being shipped out.
And-- and by that time, I knew that I would be going to the Austrian Battalion, because I didn't than go with the other guys.
So sure enough, we were shipped to Camp Atterbury in-- outside of Indianapolis.
And that was the Austrian-- there was an Austrian Battalion.

And that Austrian Battalion was the weirdest thing you can imagine because they had taken everybody from the Austrian-Hungarian Empire, not from Austria, but the old Austrian-Hungarian Empire, and put them into that-- that were not a citizen, and put them into that battalion.
And there was Archduke Otto and his brother.
And the-- kept on writing about this.
And they-- this was the idea of Secretary of War Stimson who created this thing because he felt that after the war, we would be marching into Vienna.
Archduke Otto and his brother would be marching in front of us and reestablish the old Austrian-Hungarian monarchy.
Very little is known about it.
And so the newspaper created an enormous stink.
And eventually they decided they'd have to disband the battalion.
So it was an infantry battalion.
And we trained during the winter.
It was pretty rough.
And then they gave us a choice of either to-- they didn't disband it yet.
What they did was, you had a choice, which was amazing for the Army, I think the only time it ever happened, of either going-- staying or getting into a regular infantry division.
Do you know why they decided to disband that idea?
Because there was so much adverse publicity.
And the climate was-- at that point, was so much against monarchies and establishing the old order, I guess, whatever.
So anyway, so 75% of the people signed up to get out, and-- because it was the weirdest thing.
There were university professors with four degrees, four doctoral degrees, in that battalion.
And then there were peasant farmers from Yugoslavia or from wherever, Herzegovina, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] totally illiterate.
So it was the-- the weirdest kind of combination.
And I was dying to get out.
So we-- I got shipped into the 75th Infantry Division at Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri.
Then I tried to get into-- into intelligence, into the intelligence, Army intelligence.
And in that camp was a friend of my uncle's who had-- who was in intelligence.
But it was too late already.
They had filled up the intelligence quota, and I couldn't get into it.

So I was in-- in the headquarters company, radio-- I was in headquarters company, battalion headquarters, 75th-- 290th Regiment, 75th Division, radio, Signal Division, and did my basic training there, and then was pulled out when the Army Specialized Training Program started, which was supposed to train.
They pulled out-- you had to pass all kinds of aptitude tests and took whole-day tests, different intelligence tests.
And if you passed, you went into a university.
There were three programs, engineering, medicine, and languages that they-- and if you-- if you graduated from that program, you became second lieutenant.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I passed, and knowing languages, they put me into engineering, of course, so I wound up at Iowa State and took the engineering courses and got quite a few college courses under my belt.
It was lovely.
And we were training to be officers.
And then the Battle of the Bulge came upon the United States Army.
And they felt they needed every available man.
They didn't know what was going to happen at that point.
So they stopped it, pulled everybody out.
And now I was put into signals intelligence in Camp Crowder, Missouri.
And there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- I was training in signal intelligence.
And then we were shipped to a-- I can't think of it now-- a unit outside of Washington in Virginia, which was a listening post.
And all for the-- by that time they were able to listen to all over the world, and also were teaching us Japanese code.
And I learned Japanese code.
It was called katakana.

And we were-- what the signals corps unit did, the signals intelligence unit did-- and we-- the-- the British Army won the war against Rommel in the-- in the desert by doing what we were trained to do.
They set up triangular units and they were able to establish positions, enemy positions, and-- and send enemy information or disinformation.
And that's what they did in the desert.
They set up dummy units so that he thought that the-- the regular Army units were in that area and, well, they were in other positions.
So they were able to-- it was an enormous success.
And they tried to do it so that this is what we trained for.
But then they knew we were going to enter the-- the Japanese war, so they trained us in Japanese language.
And we were all packed.
We were ready to go.
And they dropped the bomb, and we never got to Japan.
So that was my Army experience.
Then I went back to Forest Hills and lived with my mother.
And-- and then I started to go to Pratt and worked in an art studio.
How?
The news.
How did the news reach you?
Well, my-- my uncle, who lived next door, as I had said before, he was sort of the paterfamilias and he arranged for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things and did all these things that had to be done.
And he got that information.
He must have written to him, because he had contacts.
He was very active in the Jewish community in Vienna.
And he was active, had a lot of connections.
And so he-- I don't think my mother-- my mother was helping him too.
And eventually they got that letter from the-- that I-- that I showed you from the Jewish community organization in Belgrade, which notified us of my father's death.
And that was after the war, a few months after the war.
And informed the family of all the deaths that had-- my grandmother's death, and all the other family that had been killed.
About half the family had been killed.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my cousin, again, that same cousin who took me-- went with me to the train when I went into the Army.
And we were very close at that time.
We were like brothers.
He was the one who told me about my father's death.
My mother did not.
My mother just couldn't cope with it.
She couldn't cope with it.
She [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] never talked about it at all.
She never acknowledged it.
It was very difficult for me.
And I didn't talk to her, because I think we were both too afraid of talking about it.
And it was not 'til many years later, when I had worked out my own problems and I was able to talk about it and face it and what have you, that I-- eventually I said, I must open it up.
And-- and I did.
And we went to temple, to a memorial service.
And I was hoping that this would change, that now we could talk freely about it.
But she couldn't do it.
She just couldn't do it anymore.
She was totally devastated by it.
And she never-- she was a beautiful woman and she was a very smart woman.
And-- but she couldn't-- never remarried, never even went out with anybody.
Just stayed with her family.
And then, of course, when I had my own family, that was a very happy time for her, when I had my own children, and she had her grandchildren.
That was a very happy time for her.
Just that I had told a rabbi about it and, you know, saying Kaddish, and-- but--
Yeah.
It was too much.
It was her mother had been killed and her sisters had been killed.
And-- and she just couldn't, you know-- she couldn't do it.
Yeah.
I went to-- again, I worked in a-- in an art studio.
And then at night, I began to go to Pratt to get my degree.
And in those days, they did not give a full degree if you went-- went at night.
So in 1979, I went back after-- while I was running a big studio at the time.
And I went in the evening and I got my degree a year later.
Well, there was some family.
There was family here.
And matter of fact, another aunt came here who had been in Romania doing the war, and her son and eventually daughter-in-law came.
And who else was here?
Her brother was here.
So there was some family constellation.
At that time, I was still somewhat involved with my father's brothers and their children.
We were invited to their affairs and so on, actually 'til my first wife died, and then I stopped that relationship.
I didn't-- I haven't had any contact with them.
And my wife was born-- Norma was born here in the United States.
Her family was born here.
Her father and mother were born here.
And I really didn't want to-- I didn't want to speak German.
I almost never spoke German.
My older son never forgave me for that, because he said, I would know another language now.
But I just didn't want to have anything to do, you know, with Austria, with the language or what have you, the whole-- I just didn't want to know about that.
Well, even with her, I-- it took a long time.
I went into therapy and worked out my own feelings.
And-- and it-- not for quite a few years did I-- and then she became a psychotherapist eventually.
Not for many years did-- did I really discuss it.
It was very, very difficult for me to discuss it.
I think not 'til I was able to go through my own bereavement period.
Then once I did that, once I was able to go through my mourning period, then I was able to deal with-- with, you know, what had happened.
And little by little I went-- I never discussed enough of it with my children.
I certainly will make copies and give them a copy of this tape.
My younger one just asked me the other day, Dad, I told you the other day, I told you six months ago, I have to get that family tree together.
What have you done?
And I said, I haven't done anything yet.
But I will do it.
And I'll give it to both of them and try to make a family tree.
Your children-- how many children do you have?
I have two I have two children and two stepchildren.
I have four sons.
I have two sons and two stepsons.
My oldest one is Stephen and the younger one is Warren.
My stepson is Jordan, who is in Boston, and Scott, who is in Florida.
Stephen is in Boston, in the Boston area.
Warren is in Greenwich, Connecticut.
Much more, much more.
It's been a very good experience for me, because it has been-- this has been very much part of me, that whole experience.
And you can't deny it.
You can't just push it away and say it didn't happen.

And-- but I think-- I think to a lot of us, a lot of survivors and a lot of people in my situation, it took quite a few years before you could really go back again and-- and see yourself in that situation and your family, and to some extent connect with it again.
And I think it's-- it's necessary.
You must do that.
No.
No.
I have-- that-- that has been-- if I could relive it, I would do it very differently.
But I have never told them enough about it.
And I think-- I'm hoping that this will, you know, bring it up again.
I mean, now I have spoken to them over the years much more.
But I should have done more.
But I couldn't have.
I mean, 'should' is not-- you do what you can do.
And-- so-- but it was very difficult.
And it-- it leaves a scar.
It leaves a deep scar.
And you can cosmetically smooth out the top, but the-- the underneath part is there.
And you never get over that.
You never get over the losses that you have suffered.
You don't get over-- I mean, you don't get over any loss.
And I've been very lucky to have a wonderful wife now, but that doesn't mean that I don't feel the loss of my first wife who died, you know.
So-- but if you can talk about it and open it up, it helps.
My wife's name is Sylvia.
Sylvia.
You talked about your memories now and looking back.
Well, with my wife now, as I said, my first wife was born in the United States.
And there was very little-- they didn't-- weren't aware of what was going on 'til we got married.
And I mean, they knew some of it, but-- But Sylvia had a similar experience as mine.
She was born in Warsaw.
Her father was in the-- the highest official in the Polish government.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] five minutes before the German Army came into Warsaw, literally, he commandeered a car and chauffeur, which he was able to get, and said to-- to Sylvia and her mother, I'll be going into the-- he had written a book about Germany in 1933, so he was on the list.
He was on Hitler's black list and-- what was happening in Germany in '36, whenever he wrote.
And so he had to flee.
And he said, I'll go into the-- into the woods, into the forest, in the countryside.
And when this thing is over, we'll-- I'll come back.
And not expecting-- A, expecting the British to come in, as almost everybody did at that moment.
The French and the British would prevent Hitler from marching into-- into taking over Poland.
And B, also that if they were there, the Germans, they wouldn't kill people, you know.
They wouldn't kill civilians.
And so the chauffeur said to him, no, I think you're much better off.
Take your family too.
And so they packed a suitcase in 20 minutes, and they left, and eventually, after all kinds of harrowing experiences, wound up in Vilna, Lithuania.

And there Sugihara, the Japanese consul-- her father had, as being one of the-- since he had been in the Polish government, he-- Roosevelt had issued visas to-- to intellectuals, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a certain number of visas.
And he had one of those.
But he didn't have a-- a visa to-- you needed a transit visa.
Even if you had a visa to the United States, you had to have a transit visa to cross Russia to get into Japan to come to the United States.
And Sugihara provided that transit visa for them.
So excuse me, so what I wanted to say was--
--that-- that-- so we had such similar experiences, her having that-- having to go through Siberia to come here, so, you know, we talk about that constantly.
And-- and--
It's very helpful.
It's a very good thing.
No.
No.
No.
I think-- I think once or twice I might have had a dream that had some connection with that, yeah.
Maybe once or twice.
But I don't recall any particular.
Yes.
I went back in-- first time in '69.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went back again-- Sylvia hadn't seen Vienna.
We went after we went to Warsaw and we went to Auschwitz and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Birkenau.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to Vienna about six years ago.
I think at that point I had worked out most of my hatred and-- for the Austrians and for the-- that things that I-- you know, my own experiences.
And I wanted to see-- have my first wife see Vienna.
I have a-- also have a cousin in Vienna.
And so I went back for--
I went back-- at that point I said to myself, I'm going back because I want to enjoy myself and I want to do things that I'd never done as a child there, had not been able to do.
And that's what I did.
And we had a very good time.
When I-- the only unpleasant experience was when we went to a place called Bad Ischl, a spa outside of Salzburg.
And she went into a shop there, looking at handbags.
And I was waiting outside.
And there was a couple, an Orthodox couple, came walking down the street.
And she wore a sheitel and he wore a black hat but no payos And-- but it was very obvious that-- nobody in the summertime wore hats there.
It was obvious they were Jewish and a child with them.
And I was standing there, and I was sort of amazed that they were there, because it was my first time back.
And I said, why would anybody who is really Orthodox want to come here?
And then I saw an Austrian woman, elderly woman, giving them sort of dirty looks, and walking by and giving them dirty looks.
And I-- at that moment, I felt if anything had-- if anybody had said anything to them or anything had been done to them, I said, I could kill.
I could really kill.
And that was the feeling I had.
And I-- nothing happened.
She just looked at them and they walked by.
But that was the only time.
Otherwise, you know, I speak the language, and everybody was very pleasant.
The second time, when Sylvia and I went there, Sylvia didn't like the place.
I didn't like it as much either.
I sort of felt the-- the arrogance of the Austrians, what I perceived as arrogance, and also felt that nothing really had changed, that 50% were Socialists and 50% were still Nazis.
And we looked at the sites, and I said I would never go back again.
I might have to go back again next year, because my daughter-in-law's father was an architect.
He wants me to give him a tour after going to Milan, and give him a tour of Vienna.
I don't know if-- I might have to do it, but I'm not particularly-- you know, I have no feelings for it.
Yeah.
I went to my grandmother's house, which is still there.
I went to my own house.
I couldn't go upstairs.
It's now locked downstairs.
You can't walk in.
You have to have a key.
The first time I-- as I said, I-- well, you know, I had strange feelings about being back here.
And yet they were more in terms of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of relationships that were sort of-- I felt like somebody who has Alzheimer's.
Some things were still in the same place, and others weren't, and some were much larger, seemed larger, and some smaller.
And I was very disoriented.
So I felt very strange in that respect.
I had seen it as a child, and now I'm coming back and-- but the second time I think-- as I said, I went there the first time, I really wanted to just not think about that and just have a good time.
But the second time I, for instance, went back into the school.
And the school was open.
It was open because they were voting and, like here, they keep the schools open.
So I walked into the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] classroom, one of the classrooms, and they were sitting there.
And I told them I'd gone to school here 40 years ago, whatever.
And, oh, they were amazed.
They didn't ask me whether-- why I'm coming back.
And I felt totally disconnected.
The one thing-- the one experience we had, we went to a-- Could we just hold that for a second?
We're going to change the tapes for one minute.
Changing-- my [INAUDIBLE].
